content:
# Genesis Chapter 37 — The LOGOS Bible (Formal)
**Translator:** LOGOS | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios 21v–22r | **Source Images:** `source-texts/hebrew-OT/GEN_037_page_001.jpg`, `source-texts/hebrew-OT/GEN_037_page_002.jpg`
**Secondary Source:** Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), Pages 35–36 | **View LXX online:** https://digi.vatlib.it/view/MSS_Vat.gr.1209/0039

---

**1** And Jacob settled in the land of his father's sojournings, in the land of Canaan.

**2** These are the generations of Jacob. Joseph, being seventeen years old, was pasturing the flock with his brothers — he was a young man — with the sons of Bilhah and with the sons of Zilpah, his father's wives. And Joseph brought an evil report of them to their father.

**3** Now Israel loved Joseph more than all his sons, because he was the son of his old age, and he made him a long ornamented robe.

**4** And his brothers saw that their father loved him more than all his brothers, and they hated him, and they were not able to speak peaceably with him.

**5** And Joseph dreamed a dream and told it to his brothers, and they hated him still more.

**6** And he said to them, "Hear, I pray, this dream that I have dreamed:

**7** behold, we were binding sheaves in the middle of the field, and behold, my sheaf arose and also stood upright, and behold, your sheaves gathered around and bowed down to my sheaf."

**8** And his brothers said to him, "Will you indeed reign over us? Or will you indeed have dominion over us?" And they hated him still more for his dreams and for his words.

**9** And he dreamed yet another dream and told it to his brothers, and he said, "Behold, I have dreamed another dream: and behold, the sun and the moon and eleven stars were bowing down to me."

**10** And he told it to his father and to his brothers, and his father rebuked him and said to him, "What is this dream that you have dreamed? Shall I and your mother and your brothers indeed come to bow ourselves down to the ground before you?"

**11** And his brothers were jealous of him, but his father kept the matter in mind.

**12** And his brothers went to pasture their father's flock at Shechem.

**13** And Israel said to Joseph, "Are not your brothers pasturing the flock at Shechem? Come, I will send you to them." And he said to him, "Here I am."

**14** And he said to him, "Go now, see whether your brothers are well and whether the flock is well, and bring me word." So he sent him from the Valley of Hebron, and he came to Shechem.

**15** And a man found him, and behold, he was wandering in the field. And the man asked him, saying, "What are you seeking?"

**16** And he said, "I am seeking my brothers. Tell me, please, where they are pasturing the flock."

**17** And the man said, "They have moved on from here, for I heard them saying, 'Let us go to Dothan.'" So Joseph went after his brothers and found them at Dothan.

**18** And they saw him from a distance, and before he came near to them they plotted against him to kill him.

**19** And they said one to another, "Look, here comes that master of dreams.

**20** Come now, let us kill him and cast him into one of the pits, and we will say, 'A wild beast has devoured him'; and we shall see what will become of his dreams."

**21** And Reuben heard it and delivered him from their hand, and said, "Let us not strike him mortally."

**22** And Reuben said to them, "Shed no blood — cast him into this pit that is in the wilderness, and do not lay a hand upon him" — in order to deliver him from their hand, to restore him to his father.

**23** And it came to pass, when Joseph came to his brothers, that they stripped Joseph of his robe, the long ornamented robe that was on him.

**24** And they took him and cast him into the pit. Now the pit was empty; there was no water in it.

**25** And they sat down to eat bread. And they lifted up their eyes and looked, and behold, a caravan of Ishmaelites was coming from Gilead, and their camels were carrying tragacanth, balm, and laudanum, going to bring it down to Egypt.

**26** And Judah said to his brothers, "What profit is it if we kill our brother and conceal his blood?

**27** Come, let us sell him to the Ishmaelites, and let our hand not be upon him, for he is our brother, our flesh." And his brothers listened to him.

**28** And Midianite men, traders, passed by, and they drew up and lifted Joseph out of the pit, and they sold Joseph to the Ishmaelites for twenty pieces of silver. And they brought Joseph to Egypt.

**29** And Reuben returned to the pit, and behold, Joseph was not in the pit. And he tore his garments.

**30** And he returned to his brothers and said, "The boy is gone — and I, where shall I go?"

**31** And they took Joseph's robe and slaughtered a male goat and dipped the robe in the blood.

**32** And they sent the long ornamented robe and brought it to their father, and said, "This we have found. Recognize, please, whether it is your son's robe or not."

**33** And he recognized it and said, "My son's robe! A wild beast has devoured him. Joseph has surely been torn to pieces."

**34** And Jacob tore his garments and put sackcloth on his loins, and mourned for his son many days.

**35** And all his sons and all his daughters rose up to comfort him, but he refused to be comforted, and said, "No — I shall go down to Sheol to my son, mourning." And his father wept for him.

**36** And the Midianites sold him in Egypt to Potiphar, an officer of Pharaoh, the captain of the guard.

---

## LOGOS Translation Notes

### Source Manuscripts

This translation was produced from direct reading of four manuscript image sources:

1. **Leningrad Codex (Codex Leningradensis, B19A)**, dated c. 1008 CE. West Semitic Research Project photographs by Bruce E. Zuckerman. Genesis 37 spans Folios 21v–22r, across two manuscript images.

   **Folio 21v** (GEN_037_page_001.jpg): This is PDF page 41 of the West Semitic Research Project sequence — the same folio that carries the conclusion of Genesis 36 in its upper portion. The boundary between Genesis 36 and 37 is clearly visible: a parashah division marker precedes the opening words of Genesis 37:1 (וַיֵּשֶׁב יַעֲקֹב — "and Jacob settled"), with the chapter beginning well into the folio. The three-column layout with Tiberian vocalization and cantillation marks is clearly legible. The masorah parva notations appear in the inter-column margins. The manuscript carries Genesis 37 from its opening through approximately verse 22, encompassing the toledot heading (v. 2), Joseph's special garment (v. 3), the brothers' hatred (vv. 4–5), the two dreams (vv. 6–9), Jacob's rebuke (v. 10), the brothers' jealousy and Jacob's pondering (v. 11), the journey to Shechem (vv. 12–14), the encounter with the unnamed man and discovery of the brothers at Dothan (vv. 15–17), the brothers' conspiracy (vv. 18–20), Reuben's intervention (vv. 21–22). The text is legible throughout with full pointing and cantillation visible.

   **Folio 22r** (GEN_037_page_002.jpg): The recto of Folio 22. This is PDF page 42. This page carries the conclusion of Genesis 37 and the opening verses of Genesis 38. Genesis 37 concludes with the stripping of Joseph's robe and the casting into the pit (vv. 23–24), the Ishmaelite/Midianite caravan and Judah's proposal (vv. 25–27), the sale and Midianite lifting-out (v. 28), Reuben's return and despair (vv. 29–30), the bloodied robe sent to Jacob (vv. 31–33), Jacob's mourning (vv. 34–35), and the sale to Potiphar (v. 36). The three-column layout is fully legible. The masorah parva and marginal notations are visible. The name פּוֹטִיפַר (Potiphar) and the title שַׂר הַטַּבָּחִים (sar ha-tabachim — "captain of the guard") are clearly legible in the final verse. The boundary between Genesis 37 and Genesis 38 is marked by a parashah division following verse 36.

2. **Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209)** — Septuagint/LXX, 4th century CE. Two Vaticanus pages were read directly.

   **Page 35** (GEN_037_vaticanus_page_001.jpg): Vaticanus page 35 (digi.vatlib.it page pa_0039). The chapter header "CAP XLII" is visible at the top center. This page carries the end of Genesis 36 and the opening of Genesis 37 in Greek. The continuous uncial text is legible in three columns. The beginning of Genesis 37 in the LXX opens with Κατῴκει δὲ Ιακωβ ("And Jacob settled"), consistent with the MT. The page carries the text of Genesis 37 through approximately verse 22, covering the toledot notice, Joseph's dreams, and Reuben's initial intervention. The LXX rendering of key terms is visible: χιτῶνα ποικίλον for the special garment (v. 3), ἐνύπνιον for "dream" (vv. 5–9), and the brothers' speech patterns.

   **Page 36** (GEN_037_vaticanus_page_002.jpg): Vaticanus page 36 (digi.vatlib.it page pa_0040). The chapter header "CAP XLIII" is visible at the top. This page carries the conclusion of Genesis 37 and the opening of Genesis 38 in Greek. The critical second half of the chapter is legible: the Ishmaelite-Midianite passage (vv. 25–28), Reuben's return (vv. 29–30), the bloodied robe (vv. 31–33), Jacob's mourning including the Sheol verse (vv. 34–35), and the sale to Petephres (the LXX form of Potiphar) in Egypt (v. 36). The Greek text is continuous and legible throughout, with the characteristic Vaticanus uncial hand.

---

### Key Translation Decisions

**1. "These are the generations of Jacob" — אֵלֶּה תֹּלְדוֹת יַעֲקֹב (v. 2)**

The chapter opens with the standard toledot formula applied to Jacob: אֵלֶּה תֹּלְדוֹת יַעֲקֹב (elleh toledot Ya'akov — "these are the generations of Jacob"). This is the tenth and final occurrence of the toledot formula in Genesis, and it functions with extraordinary structural irony: the heading announces "the generations of Jacob," but what follows immediately is Joseph's story. Jacob is the formal subject of the genealogical heading; Joseph is the narrative subject of everything that follows through chapter 50. The toledot formula has been used throughout Genesis to introduce genealogical or biographical units (2:4; 5:1; 6:9; 10:1; 11:10; 11:27; 25:12; 25:19; 36:1; 36:9). Here it performs a pivot: the Jacob narrative formally concludes with the toledot superscription, and the Joseph narrative begins. "The generations of Jacob" are, as it turns out, primarily the story of what happened to his son Joseph — just as "the generations of Terah" (11:27) introduced the story of Abraham.

The formal translation renders "generations" throughout, consistent with all other toledot headings in the LOGOS translation. The LXX renders αὗται δὲ αἱ γενέσεις Ιακωβ (autai de hai geneseis Iakōb), consistent with the MT.

**2. "Long ornamented robe" — כְּתֹנֶת פַּסִּים (ketonet passim) (vv. 3, 23, 32)**

The phrase כְּתֹנֶת פַּסִּים (ketonet passim) is one of the most discussed translation problems in Genesis. כְּתֹנֶת (ketonet) is "robe, tunic, garment" — a long garment covering the body, the same word used in Genesis 3:21 for the garments God made for Adam and Eve. The difficulty is פַּסִּים (passim), which appears only here (vv. 3, 23, 32) and in the closely parallel 2 Samuel 13:18–19, where the same garment is worn by Tamar, King David's daughter.

The major interpretive traditions:

- **"Coat of many colors"** (KJV, 1611, following the LXX χιτῶνα ποικίλον — "ornamented/variegated tunic" — and the Vulgate tunicam polymitam — "multi-woven tunic"). This reading connects פַּסִּים with a root suggesting "stripe" or "division" and imagines a striped or multicolored garment. This rendering has dominated English-language imagination of the Joseph story for four centuries.

- **"Coat with long sleeves"** or **"long-sleeved robe"** (following the Targum Onkelos, which renders it as a "palm-length" or "wrist-reaching" garment, from פַּס meaning "palm of the hand" or "sole of the foot" — extremities, hence a garment reaching to the extremities). On this reading, the garment marks Joseph as exempt from manual labor — a robe for a child who does not work in the fields.

- **"Richly ornamented robe"** (NRSV) or **"ornate robe"** (NIV) — a general reading that acknowledges the garment's special status without committing to color or sleeve-length.

- **"Princely robe"** or **"robe of distinction"** — emphasizing its social-marking function over its physical description.

The 2 Samuel 13 parallel is instructive: the text notes that בְּתוּלוֹת הַמֶּלֶךְ מְעִילִים כַּלִּים הָיוּ לָבְשׁוֹת כִּי כֵן יִלְבְּשׁוּ בְנוֹת הַמֶּלֶךְ הַבְּתוּלֹת כֻּתֳּנֹת הַפַּסִּים ("for the virgin daughters of the king wore such garments — the royal virgin daughters were clothed in ketonet passim"). In that context it is clearly a royal garment marking high status. Joseph's ketonet passim thus marks him as his father's chosen heir, the one set apart — a visual statement of favoritism that provokes the brothers' murderous envy.

The formal translation renders "long ornamented robe" — a compound that captures both the physical distinctiveness (long, setting the wearer apart from working garments) and the decorative or status-marking character (ornamented) that the LXX's ποικίλον ("variegated, many-colored") also reflects. This is preferred over "coat of many colors" (too specifically coloristic for an uncertain term) and over "long-sleeved robe" (which privileges one etymological strand). The LXX χιτῶνα ποικίλον is noted in the variant table.

**3. "A young man with the sons of Bilhah" — נַעַר אֶת בְּנֵי בִלְהָה (v. 2)**

Verse 2 contains a syntactically dense clause: יוֹסֵף בֶּן שְׁבַע עֶשְׂרֵה שָׁנָה הָיָה רֹעֶה אֶת אֶחָיו בַּצֹּאן וְהוּא נַעַר אֶת בְּנֵי בִלְהָה וְאֶת בְּנֵי זִלְפָּה נְשֵׁי אָבִיו ("Joseph, being seventeen years old, was pasturing the flock with his brothers — he was a young man — with the sons of Bilhah and with the sons of Zilpah, his father's wives"). The word נַעַר (na'ar — "young man, boy, lad") functions here as an appositive. Some translations read it differently: "and he was a helper/servant to the sons of Bilhah" (taking נַעַר in its "attendant/servant" sense, common elsewhere). The formal translation retains "young man" as the most natural rendering, following the majority of modern critical editions, and preserves the appositive function: Joseph is specifically mentioned as pasturing with the sons of the secondary wives (Bilhah and Zilpah) — not with the sons of Leah or Rachel. This detail is subtle: Joseph (son of Rachel, the beloved wife) is out tending the flock with the secondary-wife sons, not the senior ones. The social dynamic is already complex.

**4. "His father kept the matter in mind" — שָׁמַר אֶת הַדָּבָר (v. 11)**

Verse 11 ends: וְאָבִיו שָׁמַר אֶת הַדָּבָר (ve-aviv shamar et ha-davar — "but his father kept the matter"). שָׁמַר (shamar) means "to keep, to guard, to observe, to preserve." The phrase שָׁמַר אֶת הַדָּבָר (to keep/guard the word/matter) appears in several significant Biblical contexts as a contemplative act — holding something in mind because it seems important, even if its meaning is not yet clear. The parallel in Luke 2:19 and 2:51 is famous: Mary "kept all these things, pondering them in her heart" (συνετήρει πάντα τὰ ῥήματα ταῦτα ἐν τῇ καρδίᾳ αὐτῆς) — and the Greek verb there is τηρέω/συντηρέω, itself a near-translation of שָׁמַר. Jacob hears the dream, rebukes Joseph publicly, and the brothers are filled with jealousy — but Jacob "keeps the matter." He does not dismiss it. He files it away. This quiet act of paternal pondering is the most narratively pregnant moment in the verse: Jacob suspects, even if he does not know, that his son's dream may be more than a dream.

The formal translation renders "kept the matter in mind" rather than simply "kept the matter" (too opaque in English) or "pondered the matter" (which imports more interpretive content than the Hebrew text). "Kept the matter in mind" preserves the mental/reflective dimension of שָׁמַר while remaining close to the Hebrew idiom. The LXX renders διετήρησεν τὸ ῥῆμα (dietērēsen to rhēma — "carefully preserved/guarded the word"), using a compound of τηρέω that emphasizes careful, ongoing preservation.

**5. "Tragacanth, balm, and laudanum" — נְכֹאת וּצְרִי וָלֹט (v. 25)**

The spices carried by the Ishmaelite caravan from Gilead to Egypt are listed as three items: נְכֹאת (nekho'ot), צְרִי (tseri), and לֹט (lot). These are rendered "tragacanth, balm, and laudanum" — a translation that follows the consensus of botanical and lexicographical scholarship:

- נְכֹאת (nekho'ot): A resinous gum, most likely tragacanth (from the astragalus plant), used in antiquity as a medicine, fixative, and incense component. Some identify it with storax or spicery more broadly.
- צְרִי (tseri): A healing or preservative substance often identified as balm from the terebinth or mastic tree — the famous "balm of Gilead," which appears again in Jeremiah (8:22; 46:11) as a symbol of healing. The Gilead provenance in this verse connects directly: the caravan comes from Gilead, bringing its most famous product.
- לֹט (lot): Most likely laudanum — the aromatic resin of the cistus (rock rose) plant, widely traded in the ancient Near East for medicinal and cosmetic purposes.

The formal translation prefers specific botanical identifications over vague terms like "spices" or "gum." The caravan detail is not decorative: it anchors the Joseph narrative in the real commercial world of ancient Near Eastern trade routes (the Gilead-to-Egypt route via the Jezreel corridor and Sinai was one of the most traveled in the ancient world), and it foreshadows the ultimate destination — Egypt — that will become the theater of Joseph's story. The LXX renders θυμιαμάτων ("incenses/spices"), θυμιαμάτων καὶ ῥητίνης καὶ στακτῆς — a less specific rendering.

**6. The Ishmaelite/Midianite problem (vv. 25–28)**

The most notorious textual crux in Genesis 37 is the apparent confusion between Ishmaelites and Midianites as the agents of Joseph's sale into Egypt. The text reads:

- Verse 25: a caravan of **Ishmaelites** is seen coming from Gilead
- Verse 26–27: Judah proposes to "sell him to the **Ishmaelites**"
- Verse 28: "**Midianite men, traders**, passed by, and they drew up and lifted Joseph out of the pit, and they sold Joseph to the **Ishmaelites** for twenty pieces of silver"
- Verse 36: "And the **Midianites** sold him in Egypt to Potiphar"

In verse 28, who lifted Joseph out of the pit? The subject is grammatically ambiguous: "they drew and lifted Joseph out of the pit" — the "they" could refer to the Midianites who just passed by, or to the brothers, or to the Ishmaelites. And the Midianites then sell him in verse 36, while the buyers identified in verse 28 are the Ishmaelites. The text appears to use the two names interchangeably in places, and also to distinguish them in others.

Three main explanatory approaches have been proposed:

1. **Source-critical**: The chapter combines two originally independent narratives — one (perhaps J) with Ishmaelites and Judah's role, another (perhaps E) with Midianites and Reuben's role. The combination preserves both without harmonization.

2. **Ethnographic equivalence**: In Genesis 25:1–4, Midian is a son of Abraham by Keturah; in 25:12–18, the Ishmaelites are sons of Ishmael. The two peoples were closely associated tribes in the ancient Near East and may have been used interchangeably for the trading-caravan peoples of the region. Gideon's enemies in Judges 8:24 are explicitly called both Midianites and Ishmaelites: "for they had golden earrings, because they were Ishmaelites." The names may be socially overlapping rather than mutually exclusive.

3. **Narrative reading**: The Midianites drew Joseph out of the pit (perhaps before the brothers could act on Judah's proposal) and independently sold him to the Ishmaelite caravan, which then brought him to Egypt. Verse 28 records a sub-transaction within the larger movement.

The formal translation follows the MT strictly, preserving both names as they appear without harmonization. The text's own ambiguity is theologically intentional: Joseph ends up in Egypt, sold by his own brothers for twenty pieces of silver, and the precise chain of human agents is deliberately blurred — because the narrative's ultimate claim is that God, not any human agent, was directing these events (as Joseph will declare in 45:8: "So it was not you who sent me here, but God").

**7. "Sheol" — שְׁאוֹל (v. 35)**

Verse 35 contains Genesis's first explicit use of the word שְׁאוֹל (Sheol): כִּי אֵרֵד אֶל בְּנִי אָבֵל שְׁאֹלָה ("for I shall go down to Sheol to my son, mourning"). שְׁאוֹל (She'ol) is the Hebrew term for the realm of the dead — the underworld to which all the dead descend, a place of darkness, silence, and shade. It is not equivalent to the later Christian concept of "Hell" as a place of punishment, nor to "Heaven." It is simply the dwelling place of the dead, the universal destination. The form שְׁאֹלָה (She'ola, with the directional he ending) indicates motion toward: "down to Sheol-ward."

Jacob's declaration is striking on multiple levels: (1) He presupposes that Joseph is dead — he has been deceived by the bloodied robe. (2) He says he will go "to my son" — not merely "to Sheol" — implying that the dead can encounter one another in the underworld. (3) He will go "mourning" (אָבֵל, avel) — his grief will not leave him even in death. The irony available to the reader, who knows Joseph is alive, is overwhelming: Jacob is wrong about every empirical fact, but his expression of unflinching parental devotion is exact.

The formal translation renders "Sheol" as a proper noun (not "the grave" as KJV renders it, and not "the realm of the dead" as some dynamic equivalents prefer). "Sheol" is a transliteration of the Hebrew, and its preservation is important: the word has a specific theological valence in the Hebrew Bible that "grave" obscures (a grave is merely a burial site; Sheol is a realm), and the formal-equivalence commitment requires preserving the term as it stands. The LXX renders εἰς ᾅδου (eis hadou — "to Hades"), which is the LXX's standard equivalent for Sheol — consistent in meaning but using the Greek mythological underworld term.

**8. "Potiphar, captain of the guard" — פּוֹטִיפַר שַׂר הַטַּבָּחִים (v. 36)**

The chapter closes with two interconnected identifications: the name פּוֹטִיפַר (Potiphar) and the title שַׂר הַטַּבָּחִים (sar ha-tabachim).

פּוֹטִיפַר (Potiphar) is an Egyptian name (Hebrew transliteration of Egyptian Pꜣ-dj-pꜣ-Rꜥ — "he whom Ra has given"), consistent with authentic Egyptian naming conventions of the New Kingdom period. The same name appears in variant form as פּוֹטִי פֶרַע (Poti-phera) in Genesis 41:45 for the priest of On whose daughter Asenath becomes Joseph's wife — a different individual with a similar name, a detail that has generated discussion about possible confusion in transmission but is more likely simply a reflection of the name's common use in Egypt.

שַׂר הַטַּבָּחִים (sar ha-tabachim) is literally "chief/prince of the slaughterers/butchers." The root טָבַח (tavach) means "to slaughter" — primarily animals, but also used of killing in battle (Genesis 43:16; Proverbs 7:22). In the context of royal courts, הַטַּבָּחִים (ha-tabachim — "the slaughterers") refers to the royal executioners or the royal guard — those who carry out the king's capital sentences. The title thus denotes the commander of Pharaoh's personal security and judicial execution corps — a position of enormous power and trust. The formal translation renders "captain of the guard," which has been the standard English rendering since the KJV and captures the military-judicial character of the office. "Chief executioner" would be more literal but too narrow. The LXX renders ἀρχιμαγείρου (archimageirou — "chief cook/head of the kitchen"), connecting tabachim to its basic slaughtering sense rather than its official military-judicial sense — a less accurate rendering of the Egyptian court context. The title reappears unchanged at Genesis 39:1 as Joseph enters Potiphar's household.

**9. "Evil report" — דִּבָּה רָעָה (v. 2)**

Joseph's first action in the narrative is to bring דִּבָּה רָעָה (dibbah ra'ah — "an evil report / bad report") about his brothers to their father. דִּבָּה (dibbah) specifically denotes defamatory speech, a slander or ill report — not merely neutral information. It appears in Numbers 13:32 for the "bad report" the ten spies bring back about Canaan and in Numbers 14:36–37 for the same, where it is connected to divine punishment. The formal translation renders "evil report" — preserving the moral weight of the term — rather than "bad report" (which is accurate but milder) or "tales" (too colloquial). The verse thus opens the Joseph narrative with a morally ambiguous portrait of Joseph: he is seventeen, favored by his father, reports ill of his brothers, and will be given a garment that further inflames the brothers' hatred. The narrative does not begin by making Joseph entirely sympathetic; his character is complex from the outset.

**10. "Will you indeed reign over us?" — הֲמָלֹךְ תִּמְלֹךְ עָלֵינוּ (v. 8)**

The brothers' response to Joseph's first dream deploys the infinitive absolute construction: הֲמָלֹךְ תִּמְלֹךְ (ha-maloch timloch — "to reign will you reign / will you indeed reign?") and הֲמָשֹׁל תִּמְשֹׁל בָּנוּ (ha-mashol timshol banu — "to rule will you rule / will you indeed have dominion?"). The infinitive absolute before the finite verb is the standard Hebrew construction for emphasis — it intensifies the action and, when used interrogatively as here, expresses indignant incredulity. The formal translation renders this as "will you indeed reign" and "will you indeed have dominion" — with "indeed" capturing the emphatic force of the infinitive absolute. The double question is also significant: "reign" (malach) is royal vocabulary; "have dominion" (mashal) is the verb used in Genesis 1:16 for the governing role of the luminaries and in Genesis 3:16 for the husband's rule. The brothers perceive Joseph's dream not as wishful thinking but as a claim to royal and domestic authority over them — which is why they hate him.

---

### LXX (Vaticanus) Variants — Genesis 37

Pages 35–36 of Codex Vaticanus were read directly. The continuous uncial Greek text was legible throughout both pages, with the characteristic Vaticanus hand clearly visible. The chapter number designations "CAP XLII" (page 35) and "CAP XLIII" (page 36) are visible at the top of each page. Key variants observed:

| Verse (MT) | MT (Leningrad Codex) | LXX (Vaticanus) | Translation Decision | Note |
|---|---|---|---|---|

---

### Structural and Theological Notes

**The Architecture of Genesis 37 — The Story Begins**

Genesis 37 is the opening movement of the Joseph narrative (chapters 37–50), the longest continuous prose narrative in the book of Genesis and one of the masterworks of ancient Hebrew storytelling. Its 36 verses establish every major theme, every major character relationship, and every narrative tension that will drive the next fourteen chapters. The chapter's structure can be analyzed in six literary units:

**Unit I — The Toledot Heading and Introduction (vv. 1–2a):** A two-verse frame that accomplishes a structural transition: verse 1 anchors Jacob geographically ("in the land of his father's sojournings, in the land of Canaan" — deliberately echoing the language of Isaac's sojourning in 35:27 and pointing back to Abraham's sojourning in 17:8); verse 2a delivers the toledot heading ("these are the generations of Jacob") before immediately redirecting to Joseph. The juxtaposition creates the chapter's foundational irony: this is "Jacob's story," but Jacob will spend the chapter as a passive victim of deception — it is his son's story.

**Unit II — Joseph's Privileged Status and the Brothers' Hatred (vv. 2b–11):** Three interlocking causes for the brothers' hatred are established in quick succession: (1) Joseph's evil report (v. 2b) — he is a tattletale; (2) the special garment and Jacob's favoritism (vv. 3–4) — the visual marker of hierarchy; (3) the two dreams (vv. 5–11) — the divine seal on Joseph's future elevation. The accumulation is deliberate: the hatred is not irrational or disproportionate; it is overdetermined. Each cause is sufficient; together they are explosive. The unit ends with the quiet contrast of verse 11: "his brothers were jealous of him, but his father kept the matter in mind." Jealousy versus contemplation. Fire versus patience.

**Unit III — The Journey to the Brothers (vv. 12–17):** A brief transitional episode — Jacob sends Joseph on an errand, Joseph wanders in the field, an unnamed man redirects him from Shechem to Dothan — that functions as a narrative hinge. Its structural purpose is to bring Joseph within the brothers' reach. But the episode carries hidden weight: Joseph obediently says הִנֵּנִי (hineni — "here I am"), the same word of readiness used by Abraham (22:1, 11), Isaac (22:7), and Jacob (31:11; 46:2) in moments of divine encounter and sacrificial summons. Joseph's hineni is addressed to his father, not to God — but the narrative frames him as one who, like the patriarchs before him, presents himself in readiness for whatever the journey requires. The unnamed man who redirects him ("I heard them say, let us go to Dothan") is a narratively unaccountable figure — the ancient rabbis identified him as an angel. The text leaves him unnamed and unremarked. The reader is invited to notice: Joseph was lost; something found him and sent him toward his fate.

**Unit IV — The Plot and the Pit (vv. 18–24):** The brothers' plot unfolds in two stages: first the original plan ("let us kill him and cast him into a pit," v. 20), then Reuben's intervention ("shed no blood — cast him into the pit," v. 22). Reuben's motive is given: "to deliver him from their hand, to restore him to his father" (v. 22). The brothers strip Joseph of his ketonet passim — the garment that has been the visual emblem of the enmity since verse 3 — and cast him into an empty pit with no water. The detail "no water in it" (v. 24) seems initially prosaic, but in the narrative's economy it is crucial: a dry pit is survivable. The garment is stripped; the pit is dry; survival, however improbable, remains possible. The narrator is not yet finished with Joseph.

**Unit V — The Sale (vv. 25–28):** The brothers sit down to eat — an act of chilling normalcy while their brother lies in a pit — and the Ishmaelite caravan appears. Judah's proposal is the chapter's moral pivot: not "let us free him" but "let us sell him" — upgrading the crime from murder to trafficking while framing it as mercy ("let our hand not be upon him, for he is our brother, our flesh"). The appeal to kinship is simultaneously cynical (Joseph is in a pit as they speak) and accurate (he is their brother, their flesh — which is precisely why what they are doing is monstrous). The Midianite/Ishmaelite confusion of verse 28 has been discussed above; what matters narratively is the outcome: Joseph is sold for twenty pieces of silver and is on his way to Egypt.

**Unit VI — Discovery, Deception, and Mourning (vv. 29–36):** The final movement is a triptych of responses to Joseph's disappearance. Reuben returns to the pit, discovers Joseph gone, and tears his garments (v. 29) — his plan to rescue and restore Joseph has been thwarted, perhaps by the same Midianites who acted before the brothers could sell Joseph themselves. The brothers manufacture the deception: a slaughtered goat, the bloodied robe, sent to Jacob with the question "Recognize, please, whether it is your son's robe or not" (v. 32). The word "recognize" (הַכֶּר נָא — ha-ker na) is the same verb Jacob used when he deceived his own father Isaac with the goatskins (27:23 — "he did not recognize him"), and the same verb Joseph will use to test his brothers in Egypt (42:7–8). The deception that Jacob perpetrated on Isaac is now perpetrated on Jacob. The family's wound runs both ways. Jacob's response is total collapse: he tears his garments, puts on sackcloth, mourns "many days," refuses consolation, and declares he will go to Sheol mourning. The final verse (v. 36) is a one-sentence pivot that lands Joseph in Potiphar's house in Egypt — a cliff-hanger with the widest possible gap between where the chapter opened (Jacob's household in Canaan) and where it ends (an Egyptian official's house, with Joseph as a slave).

---

**The Ketonet Passim — Garment as Theological Symbol**

The garment given by Jacob to Joseph is not merely a plot device. It functions as a concentrated theological symbol through the chapter's three appearances (vv. 3, 23, 32). Jacob gives it as an act of love and distinction. The brothers strip it from Joseph as an act of dispossession and humiliation — stripping away the visible sign of Joseph's chosen status. The brothers then dip it in goat's blood and send it to Jacob as evidence of death. In the ancient world, a man's clothing was a visible extension of his identity and his standing; to strip a man's garment was to strip his honor. The ketonet passim thus moves through three acts: gift (love), stripping (hatred), bloodied relic (deception). Each act corresponds to a relationship in the triangle: Jacob-Joseph (love), brothers-Joseph (hatred), brothers-Jacob (betrayal). The garment that marked Joseph's election becomes the instrument of his brothers' lie and his father's grief.

The theological resonance extends beyond Genesis. The motif of a garment falsely used to deceive will recur in Genesis 39:12–18, where Potiphar's wife uses Joseph's abandoned garment as false evidence of assault — again a stripped garment, again a lie to a man of authority, again Joseph condemned on the basis of cloth. Joseph's story is threaded with garments: given, stripped, falsely displayed. His ultimate rehabilitation in chapters 41–42 will be marked by the bestowal of new garments — Pharaoh's own signet ring, fine linen robes, and a gold chain (41:42) — reversing the stripping of chapter 37. The garment given and taken away in chapter 37 will be replaced by garments that no one can take from him.

---

**The Joseph Narrative and the Question of Providence**

Genesis 37 raises, without resolving, the central theological question of the Joseph narrative: how does God act in a world where human evil is operative? The chapter contains no divine speech, no theophany, no angel. God is not mentioned. What happens happens through entirely human agents: a father's favoritism, brothers' jealousy, an unnamed man's casual directions, a passing caravan, a coin transaction, a goat's blood. Yet the story is being told by a narrator who knows — and who has structured the telling so that the reader knows — that something larger is unfolding. The dreams are the initial signal: Joseph's dreams are not random; they are prophetic. The reader who knows Genesis knows that the God of the patriarchs communicates through dreams (28:12; 31:11; 46:2). Joseph's dreams will prove true. His brothers are unknowingly cooperating in the very outcome they are attempting to prevent: "we shall see what will become of his dreams," they say in verse 20, dripping with contempt. The reader already suspects — and the entire subsequent narrative will confirm — that the dreams will come true precisely because of what the brothers do in verse 20, not in spite of it.

This is the narrative logic of divine providence as Genesis 37 sets it up: not a crude mechanical determinism in which human choices are meaningless, but a sovereignty that operates through and within human choices, including evil ones. The brothers choose to sell Joseph; the Midianites choose to draw him from the pit; Judah chooses profit over fraternal murder; and every choice moves Joseph toward Egypt. Joseph will eventually articulate this theology himself: "you intended harm against me, but God intended it for good, to bring about what is now being done: the saving of many lives" (50:20). Chapter 37 is the beginning of the story that ends with that declaration. Everything in it — the garment, the pit, the twenty pieces of silver, the caravan of spices, the captain of the guard — is being woven, by the narrator, into a pattern whose meaning will not be legible until chapters 42–50.

---

**The Twenty Pieces of Silver and the Tradition of Betrayal**

Joseph is sold for עֶשְׂרִים כָּסֶף (esrim kasef — "twenty pieces of silver," v. 28). This is not a large sum: Leviticus 27:5 sets the redemption value of a male between five and twenty years at five shekels of silver; a male between twenty and sixty at fifty shekels. Twenty pieces (shekels? unspecified unit) is roughly the low-end value of a young male. Joseph's brothers value their brother at the price of a slave — a young, unmarked slave, worth the minimum. The contrast between the ketonet passim — a specially made garment, a gift of love, a costly item — and the twenty pieces of silver — the price of a disposable person — is the chapter's final irony before the story moves to Egypt.

The LXX (Vaticanus) reads εἴκοσι χρυσῶν ("twenty gold pieces") rather than silver — a variant with significant consequences for valuation (gold being far more valuable) that most scholars attribute to a scribal error in the Greek tradition (confusing χρυσός/chrysos, gold, with ἀργύριον/argyrion, silver) or to a different Vorlage. The MT's silver is followed, consistent with the price being a slave-price rather than a ransom.

The New Testament's connection of this tradition to Judas Iscariot's thirty pieces of silver (Matthew 26:15; Zechariah 11:12–13) — a close but not identical figure — was recognized in the patristic period and has remained a standard typological reading. Joseph sold by his brothers; Jesus sold by a companion; both at the price of silver; both leading ultimately through betrayal to a form of deliverance. The difference in amount (twenty vs. thirty) was treated by some patristic writers as significant (the extra ten pieces representing the additional weight of deicide), though the typological parallel does not require exact equivalence. The formal translation preserves the number and metal as the MT provides them.
